speaker: interviewer
text: --five. Survivor, Mr. Norbert Friedman. Interviewer, Mark Goldberg. We're in West Hempstead, New York, in the United States. The language is English. You were going to tell a story at the end of the last tape. Please tell that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the age of six, when we still lived in Cracow, we received a postcard from my paternal grandparents that my great-grandfather was very ill and was about to die. And he wanted to see me. The reason that he wanted to see me is because I was the first born son of a first born, the grandson of a first born, and the great-grandson of-- my great-grandfather was also first born. And therefore I was carrying the lineage of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he sent out the postcard and wanted us to come over. But in those days, you know, you didn't leave right way. The four-hour trip was as much as flying today to the West Coast. So it took us seven weeks of preparation until we finally took the train and went up the hundred miles to the town of [POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got there, my great-grandfather was in a small chamber in bed. He could hardly speak, but they brought me to his bed. And they made me put his-- put my head on the bed, and he laid his hands upon my head. And the best he could he pronounced the blessings that probably every Jewish parent pronounces upon his sons, the blessing of Menashe and Ephraim. And when he finished, he put his hand under the pillow and took an apple. Apparently he put that apple for me some four weeks ago. And by the time I got that apple, it was brown already. But I was very awed by the whole situation. And I took the apple. And I kissed my great-grandfather's hand. And after we walked out of the chamber, my grandfather said to me-- said to my father-- he says, God will bless him for that. And I always believed that my great-grandfather's blessing was the tsinnah-- was the protective shield over me in, in, in even the worst situations. We-- as I started to tell you, I went to the leadership camp on the Romanian border. Before I went to the camp, months before that, I informed my father that I will go to the camp. And my father said, agreed, OK. But when the time came, because of he needed my help in the business, he refused my leaving, and we had a falling out. And I left anyhow. And I never came back home. I stayed in Cracow, got a job, and went to work. Four weeks later, the war broke out. On September-- Friday morning of September 1, I was in the apartment of my uncle when the first Stuka planes flew over Cracow and dropped the bombs. And next to us was-- were barracks of Polish soldiers. That was the first time that I saw dead and wounded being carried out. And somehow from that day on, my world was never the same. That Friday we saw dog fights over the city of Cracow. Several Polish biplanes would get up in the air and try to fight the German fighters very unsuccessfully. There was a atmosphere of distrust. The Poles started accusing Jews of being spies for the Germans. A stranger that walked into a strange neighborhood would, would, would be accosted. All of a sudden, we found out that although for, for-- we lived in Poland for 10 centuries, we were never considered equal citizens. On Sunday, my mother came. I think she paid a lot of money and she came by bus or something from the town of Bielsko and told us about what was happening-- how the Germans were treating the young Jewish men, about the shootings and the killings in the town, and about the fact that so many of our friends and acquaintances all of a sudden declared themselves to be German in that town. And there was a council of all my aunts, and the decision was made that the young men shall flee. So myself and three of my cousins, two of whom were approximately my age. One was 10 weeks older. One was a year older. One was several years older. He already had served in the Polish Army. We decided to flee the town of Cracow. My father was still in the town of Bielsko. He tried to liquidate and realize whatever he could from whatever possessions we had. And we started out from the town of Cracow. We marched on foot toward Sandomierz and then towards central part of Poland. The scene on the highways of Poland was indescribable at that time. There was a multitude of people with whatever means of transportation they could put together-- the push carts, horses, bicycles, some very few cars. And everything was moving. They was leaving from, from-- going east, trying to escape the Germans. We were strifed and bombed by Germans' airplanes on the, on the roadsides. At night we used-- we to-- we used to try to stay with Jewish families, find lodging with Jewish families. Before the war, I refused to speak Yiddish because I considered it the language of diaspora. And all a sudden, I found myself with my only means of communication with all the Jews in the small towns and villages was the Yiddish. And all of a sudden, I had to start-- we all had to start to use it. We marched toward Sandomierz. Just before we reached the, the bridge over the Vistula River, we saw-- this was early in the morning. We saw a detachment of Polish cavalry, the Uhlani, on horses with lances and carbines, and the horses hooves wrapped in rags. They were trying to stop the advancing German motorized tank unit that was coming in that direction. Several hours later, we heard shots, machine guns. And as we later learned, supposedly the whole detachment was annihilated by the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before we were crossing the Vistula River, we were just about to reach the bridge when the tanks pulled up on the, on the-- there was an elevated part of, of, of land. And they start shelling the bridge. We managed to cross the bridge. After crossing the bridge, we encountered the headquarters of Polish Army. General Rydz-Smigly, who at that time was the Marshal of Polish Army, was bi-- bivacked over there? Is that the word correct? Bivacked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bivouacked. We tried to join the Polish Army. And we were told by the officers-- there were, there was officers were sitting at tables and signing up young Poles who want to join the army. And we tried to join. And we were told, we don't want any Jew boys in the Polish Army. And once again, you know, the reality came to play that in times of stress, somehow Jews were not Polish-- equal Polish citizens. We marched as far as a little town of-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] outside of Zamosc-- a small, little town. And there, the Germans caught up with us. And from the Germans we learned that after about three days, that the Germans gonna withdraw, and the Russians gonna occupy this part of Poland. Because there was an agreement between the Polish and the-- between the German and Russian Army as far as partitioning of Poland. And we did not want to fall into the hands of the Russians because the father of my other two cousins spent four years in the Siberian prisoner of war in World War I. And the stories that he told us convinced us that we should try to get back to Cracow. And the other reason for it was, as far as I was concerned, I had tremendous guilt feelings that I left without saying goodbye to my father. And we started on our march to-- back to Cracow. Before we left, our parents gave us addresses of relatives who lived anywhere in that part of Poland if we needed shelter. [SIGHS] We encountered villages that were burnt, houses of worship destroyed by the Germans, stories of atrocities. [? We were shown ?] a town where the house of worship was burned on, on, on Rosh Hashanah was all the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish houses of worship, or all houses of worship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. Jewish, Jewish houses of worship. The house of worship was burned on Rosh Hashanah with all the worshipers inside of it, and as well as the mikvah. I think it was in the town of Bilgoraj. I'm not completely sure. Because I know we passed the town of Bilgoraj. Either Bilgoraj or Tarnobrzeg. And one-- and one of our-- as we were marching down towards going back west, one time we stopped by the roadside in order to rest. And the convoy of Polish [? PWs ?] escorted by Germans, prisoners passed by.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Poles pointed us out to the Germans, saying [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Juden, Juden. And the officer on the horse came over to us and asked us, [GERMAN] Juden? Now there's a law in Jewish religion that forbids you to deny that you are a Jew. And as well you may-- it was well known that all laws are suspended in, in, in, in Jewish tradition when it comes to save your life, except for three laws. Only in three instances, you know, the pikuach nefesh law does not apply-- the law of saving your life. That's the instance of, of idolatry, of, of, licentious behavior, and of murder. In other words, you could not save your life and give somebody else. Because the law says, how do you know that your blood is better than somebody else? But it is forbidden by that law to deny that you are a Jew. You are supposed to suffer martyrdom before you deny that you are Jewish. So when the officer asked us, [GERMAN] Juden? Are you Jews? Automatically, we said, yes, we are Jews. And he picked us up, and we had to run in front of the horse for several kilometers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He tried to exhaust us. But then he was called to some other detail, and we ran away. That was next encounter that we had with the behavior of the Polish citizenry. [SIGHS] We arrived about two weeks later. We came back to Cracow. I slept for about 48 hours-- was out, you know-- just getting up maybe to go to the bathroom. Because I was totally exhausted from the ordeal of four weeks of marching, and covering about 700 kilometers. And the town that I came back to was not the town that I left. This is like five weeks after the war broke out. There was fear. There was mistrust. There was very feverish activity-- people trying to, to leave, to get out of the town, to go to the Russian-- those who could afford to go to the Russian-occupied zone. Because the atrocities already have started. And news of, of German behavior was also known already. And the Germans gradually instituted laws that limited activities, limited your freedom. First of all, they instituted the curfew. Afterwards, Jewish businesses were given German overseers, and then completely taken away from them. Jewish shops were closed. People were rounded up on the streets to forced labor camps or to different labor details. Then they issued a law where every Jew had to wear an armband with a, with a Star of David on it. The armband had to be a certain width and visible on your right arm. And you were not allowed to take public transportation such as trains without special permissions. Food was getting shorter supply. And the winter of 1939, 1940, was a very severe winter. And my father-- by the way, we were living with my mother's youngest sister, her husband, and her child in the apartment where my grandmother lived and the apartment that I grew up in Cracow when [INAUDIBLE] 16. And my father tried to sell whatever jewelry or whatever valuables we had in order to get food. It was getting more and more difficult. I felt it was somehow also my obligation to provide for the family. I remember a very, very cold night. The temperatures went 30 below zero centigrade. And I went and I lined up in the-- before a bakery at 6 o'clock in the evening because we understood that they were going to give out bread over there. And I stood to about 6 o'clock in the morning when they opened up the windows, and they were giving out half loaves of bread. My turn came. They closed the window, like, three people before me. And I made the decision that I have to find some other means to provide food for my family. And I got myself a pair of riding boots, a pair of riding pants. I bought on the black market a brown shirt and a belt from a German young man, from a-- with a [NON-ENGLISH] on it. And I clipped my hair very short, the way the Germans [? spoke. ?] And I went-- I used to go to buy in the German commissary. I used to walk in. I would salute, heil Hitler. And I go and purchase whatever I had to purchase. If I were-- If I was caught, there, there, there was no, you know, there was no, there was no, no court. You know, you, you were shot on the spot. But I still had to take the chance. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life was so mean that you, you, you saw instances of, of young people leaving their families in order to save themselves to go away. At that time, I joined-- my cousin enlisted me in the cell of an underground organization, the Polish Red Scout, which was a socialist underground organization. Actually what we did was we had a clandestine radio. And we printed out on onion papers the messages that came from BBC, from London, where General Sikorski tried to, you know, to lift the spirits of the Polish population, the promising-- telling us about the news of, of the front. And also the Germans, in order to, to, to bring-- to take in the currency, they decided that they're going to stamp all the high denominations bills with a special stamp. And so-- and you had only a limited amount of money that they would stamp. If you had a lot of money, you know, you, you were just-- unless you had foreign currency, the money was worthless. So we falsified the stamp, and we used to stamp the money. And we also monitored people that used to work for the Germans, in case they were traitors. I understand that the two girls that used to work in one of the German barracks that the [INAUDIBLE] the Germans were eventually executed. But we had no way of knowing. The way the cells were arranged, there were four people in the cell. You only knew only one of the people in the cell, and you-- the people from the next cell. And the cell that I was in was my cousin, I, and two brothers, two Polish fellows by the name of [? Konik. ?] And one time when I was transporting the onion papers with, with the news where there was a roundup. I was in a streetcar, and they rounded up-- they were taking people to labor, to Germany. And if I was found was that particularly newspaper with me-- you know, they were just one sheet. The news were on one sheet. And I had several sheets [INAUDIBLE] for distribution.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I hid them in the-- next to the [? motormen ?] on the, on the streetcar. And later we were all taken to the, the Gestapo headquarters for interrogation, and I received quite a beating. But I had nothing to disclose. They found these papers, but you know, they couldn't connect them to me. And I was released.

speaker: interviewee
text: In order to be able to stay in the city of Cracow, you had to have an ID called a Kennkarte. And in 1940, in order to be able to receive rations or function, this particular Kennkarte had to be stamped that you performed a certain amount, certain days of work. So during the winter, we had to shovel snow, and then they would stamp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I shoveled snow for myself, and I shoveled snow for my father, and I shoveled snow for my uncle. And the-- this was hard labor. But it allowed us to function.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day in 1940, my mother was rounded up on the street and taken-- and taken. We didn't see her till late in the morning. She, when she, when she finally came home, she was not the same person anymore. She, she was humiliated. She wouldn't tell us what they made her do. She just told us that she had to wash floors and scrub bathrooms, and she was humiliated. Whether she was physically abused or what, she would never tell us. But her whole demeanor, her whole mental and physical makeup has changed. She became very depressed. She felt she was defeated. And she insisted that we get out of the town of Cracow, that things were getting so bad that we must get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 1940, we left from Cracow. We went to the town of Tarnow. In the town of Tarnow, my father was able to make connection with a big marmalade factory that was under German supervision as a supplier of fruit. And he traveled to, you know, to a certain part in the countryside buying up fruit for the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in order to bring that food in, it had to be either brought in by trucks or put on the train loads. And you had to-- you had to be able to, you know, to navigate. And in order to navigate, you had to have papers. And so I acquired some papers, false papers, Aryan papers. And I used to go to the town-- to the villages where my father used to purchase, make sure that stuff was loaded and then delivered to Tarnow. One day in Tarnow, I was stopped in the street. And this was after the curfew. I had took a girl home, and I was going back to the place we lived. And I took my armband off because it was after curfew. And I was accosted by, by drunken men who spoke broken German and required-- asked me for papers. And I started yelling at him in German. And some two German gendarmes came over. And eventually I was taken to the Polish police station. It turned out that the man was the labor commissar for the Germans. He was a ethnic German. And I didn't show him the papers because I was afraid, you know, that they would investigate. They'd make me drop the pants, and they would know that I was Jewish. Jews were the only circumcised people in Poland. Non-Jews were not circumcised in Poland. So I was sent to a labor camp in Pustkow. The labor camp in Pustkow at that time-- the labor consisted of building barracks. We did not know at that time. But later on, in that particular area, they built prototypes for V1. And they, they had about 40,000 of Russian prisoners of war in that particular camp later. But we were, at that time, building barracks. And the labor was very hard. Conditions were very difficult. And we worked under the supervision of Polish civilian workers who used to come in the morning and leave in the evening. And I made the decision one day that I'm going to walk out from this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our clothing, our jackets were painted with a, with a yellow stripe and a KL sign on it which indicated it was konzentrationslager or arbeitslager. And so I hid my jacket, and I stole a jacket from a Polish worker. And a couple days later, I stole a [? mess kit. ?] And I [? pulled ?] [? out ?] the jacket and [? the mess kit ?] and I walked out with the Polish workers.

speaker: interviewee
text: They checked their papers, you know, every fourth, fifth man. And it was just a question of whether I'd be lucky enough or not. And I was able to walk out. And I had no money because they took all your possessions away when you got to camp. And I got on the, on the train to go back to Tarnow. And I had to ride outside of the train so the conductor couldn't check my, my, my, my ticket. And when I got to Tarnow, my father, during his travels, found a little village. He told us that he found a little village outside Sandomierz called Zawichost, where he felt we would be safer than in Tarnow. My mother refused to go at first. And I went over there. And then later my mother joined us. My little brother, at that time, all the time was still with my grandparents. Because I told you at the beginning in summer of 1939 he went to live with my paternal grandparents. When the war broke out, they, they took the train back to the town where my father was born which was the town where they originally came from, and my brother, my little brother stayed with them. As a matter of fact, in the winter of 1940-- it was a very severe winter when the-- when food was very difficult to get-- a decision was made that my mother, I, and another neighbor of ours shall go to the village where my grandparents, paternal grandparents, lived and we'd buy some food to bring back to town. And we set out on the train. We went to the town of Debica and from there we hired out a horse and a wagon. And it was so cold that we ran most of the time behind the horse and the wagon-- a sled, actually, not a wagon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to the little village of Wielopole where my grandparents lived and my little brother was. And we started buying out, buying up food. And my little brother was, by that time, nine years old. 1940? 8 years old. And he was quite proficient in, in, in going to the villages and buying poultry and eggs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we bought enough food to take with us. We bought butter. We bought some geese that we had slaughtered. Anyhow, the three of us, we packed the food, and we hired a sled to go back to, to the railway station. And we made the decision not to go to the main railway station, but to go to a minor station. Because the German soldiers wouldn't be there and they wouldn't be checking. We set out at night. And it was a, a full moon, completely cloudless night. And we were on this sled. And the, the wolves from the forest which we were passing by scared the horses. And the sled went into the ditch, and we couldn't dig out. And at night, the landscape was something like you see in winter landscape. That's what it looked like. Nothing-- you could see nothing but snow and the moon shining. We finally found a little hut where a Polish peasant helped us out and pulled out this sled out on the road. And we went to the train station. When we got on the train, 10 minutes later, some German gendarmes came over and confiscated all the food that we had with us. It was one of the reasons also that we decided to get out from Cracow and go into a smaller town. Eventually, as I said, my mother moved to the town of Zawichost. And at that time, the, the Germans ceased-- you know, they didn't give my father the permits or the orders to buy the-- they completely excluded the, the Jewish previous owners. Because a ghetto was being formed in Tarnow. And, and my father lost the contact. And he had to look for some other means of support. Since he was the only kosher butcher in that little town that we lived in who was willing to, to risk his freedom and life and slaughter, and slaughter, which was illegal, he engaged into, in the butchering. This is what he was doing. Later on, in 1942, in May of '42, an informer, a, a chimney sweeper who was an informer for the Germans-- because he traveled to the villages, he knew what was going on-- informed on, on my father that he was slaughtering non-kosher meat. They caught the man who was distributing the meat in the villages. They brought him to our house. They shot him in front of the house. I grabbed my father through the house. We ran down the hill to the Vistula River. I knew a friend of mine had a boat parked, a little wooden boat. And I put my father in the boat. And there were no oars. There was just a long stick that you maneuvered the boat across the river. And I took my father across the Vistula River. And I hid him in the forest. And I returned back to the town to confer with my mother what we should do. And the decision was made that we have to get out of town. My mother was not in the house at that time, so she was able to stay with some friends. OK, we'll have to stop there--
